_meta.description: Founding brand-pet cast for Little Souls FB content. Each cast member has a locked identity (master image where available), breed-specific keep_list for prompt anchoring, story anchors drawn from a Habit Pool (per littlesouls-angle-prompt skill), and signature canvas style. Codex passes the master image as reference + the keep_list in every prompt to lock identity across multiple generations.

clover.name: Clover
clover.personality: The rescue who took three months to bond. Watchful, soft, slow to trust then absolute. Sleeps under her portrait now.
clover.keep_list: feathered tail, soft sable-and-gold layered coat, narrow muzzle, dark almond eyes, slight head-tilt, kind set of the brow, white wisp on chest only
story_anchors: the kitchen radiator she claimed within an hour of getting home
didn't whine the first night — just blinked at the new dark like she was already counting it as hers
bit two foster carers in the first week
spent three months sleeping in the corner of the kitchen, watching the door
sleeps under her portrait on Sundays now
found wildflowers in the garden on her first proper walk
carries socks to the door but never out of it
the soft light by the kitchen window she always finds
breed_facts: Goldens hit their adult coat around fourteen months — soft puppy down replaced with the feathered legs that's the breed standard.
Lord Tweedmouth bred the first one in the Scottish Highlands in 1865. He wanted a retriever that wouldn't break the bird.
A Golden's mouth is so soft they were trained to retrieve eggs without cracking them.
The breed standard calls for 'eager, alert and self-confident' — three words that explain why they sit at the door at 5pm.
Goldens were almost extinct after WWII. Three breeders kept the line going.
They're one of only six breeds developed specifically for the comfort of the human carrying the gun.

atlas.name: Atlas
atlas.personality: Kitchen-window-watcher. Aloof until he isn't. Bonded the day he stopped pretending not to be.
atlas.keep_list: ink-black coat, single small white wisp on chest only, pale green-yellow eyes, tall ears with slight forward-set, narrow body, long whip tail
story_anchors: watches the kettle boil from the kitchen window every morning
knows when you're crying before you do
sleeps on the warm laptop and refuses to move
small white wisp on the chest is the only un-black thing about him
stalked his own reflection for the entire first week
claimed the second step of the stairs because he could see the front door AND the kitchen at once
drops things off the table deliberately and watches you pick them up
purrs only when you're sad
breed_facts: Black cats lose their kitten eye-blue around twelve weeks. The pale yellow or green that comes in is theirs for life.
Cats have 32 muscles in each ear and can rotate them 180 degrees independently.
Cats domesticated themselves around 9000 years ago — earliest evidence is a 7500 BC burial site in Cyprus.
A cat's purr sits between 25 and 50 hertz. The same range used in bone-regrowth therapy studies.
Cats recognise their names. They just choose whether to respond.
Black cats have a single dominant gene (TYRP1). Statistically more melanistic cats are male than female.

hazel.name: Hazel
hazel.personality: Grey muzzle, sleeps under the kitchen table. The gentlest archetype for memorial framing only. Never used for active grief — only senior remembrance.
hazel.keep_list: grey muzzle fading into chestnut coat, soft eyes with the slight cataracts of age, broad warm pink belly, paws white-tipped, the slowness of a senior dog
story_anchors: sleeps under the kitchen table where it's coolest
took a year at the rescue before she wagged her tail again
still flinches at raised voices, six years later
drops her head into your lap when you're crying
the slow, deliberate way she follows you from room to room
the way she sighs when she lies down — long and final
the white that's spreading from her muzzle up around her eyes
breed_facts: Senior dogs grieve too — documented behaviour change in canines after losing a household member, human or animal.
Lab mixes are the most common breed in UK shelters; they're also the ones that bond hardest after long stays.
A dog's sense of smell is 40 times more powerful than ours, which means the absence of someone is something they detect long before we know to ask.
Cataracts in older dogs don't always mean blindness — many dogs adapt by mapping the house through smell and sound.
hazel.ethics_note: Hazel is fallback only. Real customer memorial submissions take Bridge Friday whenever available. Never use Hazel for a 'just lost' framing — only 'gentle remembrance' or 'senior dog in the family' archetype. Five Love Test must pass.

beans.name: Beans
beans.personality: Snorts at thunder. Fierce about toys. Will wear any costume; resents none of them.
beans.keep_list: fawn coat with black mask, bat ears at full alert, comical underbite with tiny lower teeth visible, brindle dappling on front paws, expressive bottom lip, slightly bowed front legs
story_anchors: snorts at thunder but not at fireworks
stole a sock and slept with it for three days
won't share the bed; will share the chair
her bat ears swivel independently like radar
the comical underbite means her tongue lives slightly out at all times
ferocious about her squeaky lobster
trembles at the bath, doesn't trembles in the rain
breed_facts: French Bulldogs were bred by lace workers in Nottingham in the 1800s as ratters and lap dogs. They went to Paris with the workers when textile work moved.
Their ears are a 'bat ear' set — a deliberate breed standard adopted in Paris in 1898.
Frenchies cannot swim — their dense bone structure and short snout mean they sink. Most pet insurance excludes water claims for the breed.
Their underbite is functional, not cosmetic — it lets them grip without hurting their soft palate.
About 80% of French Bulldogs are born by C-section because of their head-to-pelvis ratio.

mochi.name: Mochi
mochi.personality: Older than the breed. Aristocratic indifference. Watches the window for hours and reports back nothing.
mochi.keep_list: thick mane around face, prominent ear tufts, slow-blink amber eyes, long ringed tail thicker than her body, fur cascading in distinct layers, large paws with tufted toes
story_anchors: watches the window for hours and reports back nothing
drops things off the table deliberately, then walks away
the slow-blink that says I will hear you out, briefly
her tail is bigger than her body when she's annoyed
claims the highest point in any room — top of the wardrobe by default
purrs at a register low enough to feel in your sternum
the way she walks past you like she's running for office and you didn't sign her petition
breed_facts: Maine Coons are the largest domestic cat breed — males commonly hit 9 kilos.
A coin from 1500 BC at the British Museum has a cat on it with the same eyes, chest, and ear tufts as a Maine Coon. Same breed, four millennia ago.
They're one of only two cat breeds with tufted toes — the other is the Norwegian Forest Cat.
Maine Coons trill more than they meow. The trill is a greeting; the meow is a complaint.
Their ear tufts (called 'lynx tips') are a thermal regulation adaptation — they evolved in Maine winters.
They take four years to reach full size. Most cats finish growing at one.
